titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Langhorne Gibson Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Ruth Gay and Carol King

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

conventiondeclaration.abbr: Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual
conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday September 2, 2020 at 10:37

did.abstract: The papers consist of pamphlets and other printed matter with some letters, diaries and manuscripts relating to naval affairs. The largest block of material concerns World War I, particularly the questions of the German naval mutiny of 1917, the surrender of the German fleet in November 1918, and the scuttling of its most important units at Scapa Flow in 1919. Also in the collection are circa two thousand postcards showing World War I naval scenes and photographs of American transports.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Accession 1992-M-004 was transferred from the Bibliography Department in 1991.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Langhorne Gibson Papers (MS 237). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Libary.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Langhorne Gibson graduated from Yale College with the Class of 1922. He was apprenticed with the Crowell Publishing Company in New York City, and was author of Death of a Fleet and The Riddle of Jutland . Gibson's hobbies were hunting and yachting, and he designed and built Diana, a motor-sailer on which he and his wife lived during the first year of their marriage. He married Parthenia Ross in 1936 and they had seven children.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers consist of pamphlets and other printed matter with some letters, diaries and manuscripts relating to naval affairs. The largest block of material concerns World War I, particularly the questions of the German naval mutiny of 1917, the surrender of the German fleet in November 1918, and the scuttling of its most important units at Scapa Flow in 1919. Also in the collection are circa two thousand postcards showing World War I naval scenes and photographs of American transports.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The records are arranged by accession.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright status for collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Log book of the Yacht Diana , from Nassau to the Solomon Islands, 1934-1935.
Specifications for the construction of the Yacht Diana .

did.unittitle: Calendar of papers delivered by Captain L.L. Black, U.S.N., to Robert F. Metzdorf

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to General Wright. 1p. 21 Feb. 1888.
Social note, asking him to visit.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to Robert Kennedy. 1p., address on p. 4. 17 Jun. 1822.
He forwards an order from the Navy Department.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to G.C. Brevoort. 2p. 30 Sept. 1883.
He gives details about cataloguing the late Mr. Nurphy's library.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to Maj. John Symington. 2p. 17 May 1835.
He orders equipment to be sent to St. Louis from the Watervilet Arsenal. [Transferred to Beinecke, Western Americana.]

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to James Grant Wilson. 1p. (double sheet) 14 Feb. 1902.
He thanks him for an article.

odd.head: General
odd.p: .L.S. to Amos Binney. 1p., address on p. 4. 21 Oct. 1815.
He orders the gig sent for the Franklin and inquires about cabin furniture for the Independence .

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to P.P. Blair. 1p., address on p. 4. 5 Nov. 1832.
He encloses $5.00 for a subscription to The Globe .

odd.head: General
odd.p: L.S. to unidentified recipient. 1p. 22 Oct. 1842.
Inquires about felt required for covering shot, in experimenting with Captain Stockton's gun.

did.unittitle: Engle, F., Capt., U.S.N. New York.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to A.O. Dayton. 1p. 20 Jan. 1858.
He inquires about a new pay schedule; Commodore Paulding sends regards.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to Gideon Welles. 2p. (double sheet) 23 Jun. 1866.
He acknowledges receipt of a pending bill and a copy of the Register; he will be glad at all times to advise about naval affairs.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Signature, clipped from a letter.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to F.L. Seeley. lp. 21 May 1926.
Letter of thanks for courtesies extended.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to Mr. Gallup. 1p. (double sheet) Monday morning, no year.
Urges him to disregard Nicaraguan reports "based on dirty American dollars."

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to Samuel L. Southard. 1p., endorsed on p. 4. 12 May 1825.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to Alexander Parris. 1p. 19 Dec. 1835.
He has received the accounts and will write as soon as the Auditor has given a decision.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to Governor Hoppin. 2p. (double sheet) 28 Mar. 1856.
He hopes to arrange a meeting; he has sent him a copy of an article. [Missing, 12/79]

did.unittitle: Philip, John W. U.S.S. New York , off Tompkinsville.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to Willam D. Murphy. 3p (double sheet) 4 Nov., no year.
He regrets his inability to accept an invitation; he will be at sea.

odd.head: General
odd.p: L.S. to Mrs. Daniel Ammen. 1p. 29 Feb. 1868.
He forwards a letter received from Captain Ammen.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to H.B. Dawson. 4p. (double sheet) 15 Oct. 1872.
He gives details about his publications; he will argue his restoration of rank before the next session of Congress.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to Charles Wilkes. 1p., with address on p. 4. 21 Apr. 1838.
He will await his arrival in Baltimore and discuss the refitting of the brig. [Transferred to Beinecke, Western Americana.]

odd.head: General
odd.p: A.L.S. to the Rev. Mr. Lawrence F. Bower. 2p. (double sheet), undated.
He gives details of the commanding officers at the Tutuila Naval Station.

odd.head: General
odd.p: T.N.S.,18 Apr. 1933, to accompany printed muster roll of the U.S.S. Noma , 1917-1918.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Diary, 1914-1918, on board the S.S. Port Lincoln .

odd.head: General
odd.p: Notebook [apparently compiled in 1811] in two or more unidentified hands containing: I. Renseignements sur la Mer Noire, 1809, 102 p. in French. 2. A general statement concerning the export and import trade of Russia. 3. Note on the Mediterranean trade as connected with Russia. Also five other similar essays. Bound in 1 vol.

did.unittitle: Cruise of the Prinz Eitel Friedrich by Fritz Runge

odd.head: General
odd.p: Typescript, 198 p. Note by Gibson says mss is badly written and padded, that he purchased it with understanding it was not to be published.

did.unittitle: 1. English translation of excerpts from Der Grosse Kreig 1914/18 , edited by M. Schwarte, typed.
2. Put out your boiler fires, a historical play by Ernst Toller, translated from the German, typed.
3. Christmas eve, 1914. (Memoirs of an officer of the Imperial Russian navy.) Signed A. Loukine.
4. The battle of the Bosphorous. (Russian pre-dreadnoughts and the "Goeben" in 1915.) Signed S. Loureine.
5. The submarine mine-layer Crab on service in the great war. (Memoirs of an officer of the Imperial Russian navy.)
Miscellaneous concerning the surrender of the German fleet
1. 4 pages cut out from The Wonder of the War at Sea by Holt and Wheeler.
2. 8 pages cut out from The Great War—The Triumph of Democracy
3. 14 pages cut out from Dress of the Day , a novel by a British R.N. Surgeon.
4. Notes on the Von Meurer Conference, held on the 15th and 16th of November, 1918; accompanied by a letter from an Admiralty clerk.
5. Blue Jackets of 1918 , by Willis J. Abbot, typed mss.
6 memoranda from Navy Department, Office of Naval Operations, Washington, concerning German vessels
8. Ex-German fleet, how disposed of. Extract from Brassey's Annual
9. Present position of the German war ships that have been salved at Scapa Flow
10. Surrender of the German fleet, by Capt. Schnell, German navy (ret.) source. German officers' journal.
11. Copy of the order which Meurer sent via radio to the commander-in-chief after his conference with Beatty.
12. The German battleship Baden from Surrendere d by Griff. Typewritten transcript.
13. "Conclusions" from Merchantmen-at-Arms by David Bone. Typed transcript.
14. U.S.S. Ostfriesland , at sea, enroute to N.Y. Report of the commanding officer to Navy Department, re: ex-German ships, preparation of cruise to the U.S.
15. Extract from The British Submarine in Being by J.A. Blackburn and Kenneth Watkins, London, Chapter XI "Fritz," Chapter X "Der Tag"
1. The trouble in the fleet (1917). Trans. from Der Marxismus und das Deutsche Heer im Weltkreig by Major E. O. Volkmann, Berlin.
2. Vidil, Charles: Mutinies in the German Navy , p. 104, typed translation
3. Germany—Mutinies in the navy and shipyards. Naval intelligence report.
4. Germany—Disciplinary measures against mutineers. Naval intelligence report.
5. Mutinies and outbreaks at Kiel and Flensburg. Naval intelligence report.
7. 1917 Mutiny in the German fleet. A copy of a secret British bulletin sent to U.S. Navy at the time.

2. Includes scrapbook containing clippings, photographs, typescripts and documents relating mainly to naval affairs, 1917-1936. Most of the correspondence was originally filed in this scrapbook and is now in box 1.
3. Miscellaneous papers removed from the scrapbook.
The Firth and the Scapa Flow

odd.head: General
odd.p: Typescripts of articles and dispatches from the London Times , 8 Nov 1918- 11 Jan 1930.

did.unittitle: The Firth and the Scapa Flow

odd.head: General
odd.p: Typescripts of articles and dispatches from the New York Times , 1918 Nov-1920 May 15.

did.unittitle: Postcards and photographs of World War I naval scenes

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Transferred from the Bibliography Department, Sterling Memorial Library, 1991 Jul 26

did.unittitle: Photographs of American transport ships used during World War I
